IsA: It is a subtype of mancala games.
HasA: It has carved wooden elongated boat-shaped boards.
It has cup-shaped holes.
It has two sets of seven holes for each player.
It has two larger holes at each end which are known as the stores.
It has seeds or counters.
It has cowrie shells, pebbles, or tamarind seeds.
It has holes called lubang in Malaysia.
It has store holes called rumah in Malaysia.
It has holes called anak in Indonesia.
It has store holes called indung in Indonesia.
HasFeatureOf: It is characterized by the player's store being included in the placing of the seeds.
It is characterized by variations in rules and number of holes used.
It is characterized by names that are likely onomatopoeiac.
It is characterized by names that have come to mean cowrie shells.
It is characterized by holes that are typically deeper and larger than variants in mainland Asia and Africa.
It is characterized by a total of 98 pieces used in the seven-hole board version.
It is characterized by holes called bahay or balay in the Philippines.
It is characterized by store holes called ulo in the Philippines.
It is characterized by rules that are almost identical in Malaysia, Brunei, Indonesia, the Maldives, Marianas, and the Philippines.
It is characterized by the objective to capture more seeds in the store than one's opponent.
UsedFor: It is used for developing certain mathematical principles.
It is used for play between two people.
LocatedIn: It is located in Southeast Asia.
It is located in Malaysia.
It is located in Indonesia.
It is located in the Philippines.
It is located in Brunei.
It is located in the Maldives.
It is located in the Marianas.
It is located in Thailand.
It is located in Sri Lanka.
It is located in India.
BuiltIn: It was built in the 4th century AD.
PublishedIn: It was published in Dutch sources.
ConsistsOf: It consists of boards with cup-shaped holes.
It consists of seeds or counters.
It consists of cowrie shells, pebbles, or tamarind seeds.
It consists of seven seeds placed in each small hole.
It consists of a player's own store.
It consists of an opponent's store.
PartOf: It is part of mancala games.
It is part of Southeast Asian mancalas.
KnownFor: It is known for variations in rules and number of holes.
It is known for its names being variations of similar cognates.
It is known for its names referring to cowrie shells.
It is known for its holes being deeper and larger than variants in mainland Asia and Africa.
It is known for being useful for developing mathematical principles.
It is known for being featured on the reverse of the Malaysian Ringgit 10 sen coin.
AssociatedWith: It is associated with Austronesian trading routes.
It is associated with South Asia.
It is associated with the game of mancala.
It is associated with cowrie shells.
It is associated with pebbles.
It is associated with tamarind seeds.
It is associated with ceremonies to propitiate ancestors.
It is associated with prehistoric dakon stones.
It is associated with cup-shaped depressions in other Austronesian cultures.
It is associated with Polynesia.
OccurredIn: It occurred in Southeast Asia.
It occurred in Malaysia.
It occurred in Indonesia.
It occurred in the Philippines.
It occurred in Brunei.
It occurred in the Maldives.
It occurred in the Marianas.
It occurred in Thailand.
It occurred in Sri Lanka.
It occurred in India.
InfluencedBy: It was influenced by Austronesian trading routes.
It was influenced by South Asia.
Influenced: It influenced the Malaysian Ringgit 10 sen coin.
